title: Contribute
text:
Contribute

PySAM SysML2 is a Python library that makes accessing the SysML V2 standard API easier. It is developed by the R&D team dedicated to Ansys SAM. For now, the only tested implementation of the SysML V2 standard API is the one proposed by SAM.

Contributing to PySAM SysML2 is welcomed and can be in the form of discussions, code,
documentation, or issue reports.

Overall guidance on contributing to a PyAnsys library appears in the
Contributing topic
in the PyAnsys developer’s guide. Ensure that you are thoroughly familiar
with it and all style guidelines before attempting to contribute to PySAM SysML2.

The following contribution information is specific to PySAM SysML2.

title: Contribute > PySAM SysML2 documentation
text:
PySAM SysML2 documentation

Documentation for the latest stable release of PySAM SysML2 is hosted at
PySAM SysML2 documentation.

This version is automatically kept up to date with GitHub actions.

title: Contribute > Posting Issues
text:
Posting Issues

Use the PySAM SysML2 Issues
page to submit questions, report bugs, and request new features. When possible,
use one of the existing templates.

To reach the project support team, email pyansys.core@ansys.com.

title: Contribute > Contribute code
text:
Contribute code

As PySAM SysML2 is associated with the SysML V2 standard API and strongly related to SAM,
any contribution is analyzed and possibly integrated by the SAM development team.

title: Contribute > Source code
text:
Source code

Run this code to clone and install the latest version of PySAM SysML2 in development mode:

title: Contribute > Code style
text:
Code style

PySAM SysML2 follows the PEP8 standard as outlined in the PyAnsys development guide. Code style is enforced using Ruff for linting.

title: Contribute > Testing
text:
Testing

PySAM SysML2 uses pytest.
In the main directory, run this command:

Tests are in the tests folder. Add your own tests for non-regression.

To run tests with coverage:

This generates a coverage report in htmlcov/index.html.

title: Contribute > Documentation
text: Documentation

title: Contribute > Build the documentation
text:
Build the documentation

PySAM SysML2 uses Sphinx for documentation generation. To build the documentation:

To generate a PDF version:

The generated HTML documentation is in the doc/_build/html directory
and can be viewed locally:

Then open your browser to http://localhost:8000.

The PDF is generated in the doc/_build/latex directory.

title: Contribute > Run Vale
text:
Run Vale

Vale is used to enforce documentation style guidelines and ensure consistency across the documentation.
It checks for grammar, style, and terminology compliance with the Google developer and
Ansys style guides.

Before running Vale, you must download the style definitions:

This downloads the required style guides defined in the .vale.ini file

To check documentation files:

Vale reports any style violations, spelling errors, or inconsistencies. Fix these issues before submitting documentation changes.

title: Contribute > Pre-commit hooks
text:
Pre-commit hooks

PySAM SysML2 uses pre-commit hooks to automatically check code quality before each commit.
Pre-commit runs various checks including code formatting, linting, and style validation.

To install the pre-commit hooks:

Once installed, the hooks run automatically when you run the git commit command. The pre-commit configuration is defined in the .pre-commit-config.yaml file.

To manually run all pre-commit hooks on all files:

If a hook fails, fix the reported issues and commit again. Some hooks (like ruff) can automatically fix issues.

title: Contribute > Quality checks workflow
text:
Quality checks workflow

Before submitting a pull request, ensure all quality checks pass:

Run tests: pytest

Run tests with coverage: pytest --cov=ansys.sam.sysml2 --cov-report=html

Check code style: ruff check .

Format code: ruff format .

Check documentation style: cd doc; vale sync; vale .

Run pre-commit checks: pre-commit run --all-files

The CI/CD pipeline automatically run these checks on multiple Python versions (3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14) when you submit your pull request.

While you can run tests locally on your installed Python version, the CI/CD pipeline ensures compatibility across all supported Python versions.

title: Simplify SAM project initialization
text:
Simplify SAM project initialization

This example shows how to use the simplified
AnsysSysML2Project class
to work with a SysML v2 project on SAM. This approach reduces boilerplate code by automatically
initializing all necessary connectors. It explains how to perform these tasks:

Simplify project initialization with a single class.

Download diagrams (single and batch).

Create new elements.

Navigate through project diagrams.

Retrieve diagram information.

The
AnsysSysML2Project
class is specifically designed for SAM projects and automatically handles the initialization
of these classes:

AnsysSysML2APIConnector,

SamRestApiConnector,

SysML2ProjectManager,

SAMDiagramManager.

title: Simplify SAM project initialization > Prerequisites for simplified SAM
text:
Prerequisites for simplified SAM

Ensure that you meet these prerequisites:

A running SAM server instance.

A valid organization ID, project ID, and token.

The bike.xmi model imported into your project.

title: Simplify SAM project initialization > Simplified Python example
text:
Simplified Python example

Simplified example using AnsysSysML2Project for SAM projects

title: Simplify SAM project initialization > Key advantages
text:
Key advantages

Compared to the traditional approach described in
Download diagrams and create new elements, using the simplified
AnsysSysML2Project class
offers these advantages:

Single initialization: One class automatically handles all connectors.

Built-in diagram management: Removes the need to manually create and manage the
SAMDiagramManager
class.

Streamlined API: Provides direct access to project operations without managing multiple
connector instances.

Integrated features: Combines project management, diagram operations, and element creation in
one interface.

Replace placeholder values with your actual SAM configuration.

The AnsysSysML2Project
class automatically manages diagram loading and the connector lifecycle.

The class supports all diagram formats (png, svg, jpeg) for downloads.

title: Calculate computer cost
text:
Calculate computer cost

Download the computer model used in this example and import it into a new project to work on.

Download the model: Computer Model.

Open the SAM editor in your browser and select the desired organization (for example, MyOrga).

Select New Project > SysML V2 > Import File.

Select Choose File for the File to import option.

Select the computer.xmi file that you just downloaded. The project name is automatically
set to computer.

Click Import and wait for the project to load.

You can now work on this computer model.

title: Calculate computer cost > Calculate cost
text:
Calculate cost

You need to replace the organization ID, server URL, and token with your own data. For more
information, see Find information.

title: PySAM SysML2 documentation (v 0.1.0)
text:
PySAM SysML2 documentation (v 0.1.0)

PySAM SysML2 provides a Python scripting interface for SysML2 models. It loads models from any
SysML2 tool that implements the standard API. The loaded model maps into a Python object, allowing
you to manipulate, browse, and edit it. You can then push modifications back to your modeling tool.

PySAM SysML2 works with the Ansys SysML2 modeling tool,
Ansys System Architecture Modeler (SAM).

Getting started 

Explains how to install PySAM SysML2 and set up a project.

getting_started/index

User guide 

Explains how to connect to PySAM SysML2, work with models and diagrams, and find
required information.

user_guide/index

API reference 

Describes PySAM SysML2 functions, classes, and methods.

api/index

Examples 

Provides some examples of how to use PySAM SysML2.

examples/index

Contribute 

Learn how to contribute to the PySAM SysML2 codebase
or documentation.

title: Find information
text:
Find information

This page explains how to find required information, such as the project ID or your authorization
token.

title: Find information > Find project ID
text:
Find project ID

You can find the project ID in different places.

Editor:
Open the project in the editor. The project ID appears in the web page URL:

Dashboard:
Open the project view page. The project ID appears in the web page URL:

title: Find information > Find organization ID
text:
Find organization ID

Follow these steps to find your organization ID:

Go to your dashboard.

Click your organization.

Find your organization ID in the page URL:

title: Find information > Find authorization token
text:
Find authorization token

For authorization, you can either use a bearer (temporary) token for quick access or create a
Personal Access Token (PAT) for longer-term use.

title: Find information > Bearer token
text:
Bearer token

Follow these steps to get a bearer token:

Open the SAM editor dashboard in your browser.

Press F12 to open the browser’s console.

Go to the Network tab and reload the page.

Many requests appear. Search for one of these:

me

organizations

access-rights

Click the relevant request and go to the Headers tab.

The Authorization header shows the value of your bearer token.

Copy only the token after Bearer.

title: Find information > Personal Access Token (PAT)
text:
Personal Access Token (PAT)

Creating a Personal Access Token (PAT) is the recommended method for authentication with
PySAM SysML2 due to its longer validity.

New method (recommended for current servers)

Follow these steps to generate a PAT from your account settings:

Access account settings:
From the main page of the SAM editor, click your account logo in the top right corner and select Account settings.

Navigate to personal access tokens:
On the Account settings page, click Personal access tokens in the navigation pane.

Generate new token:
Click the Generate new token button.

Configure token details:
Enter a descriptive label for your token in the Token label field and set the expiration date. Click the Generate token button.

Copy generated token:
When the token displays, click Copy to save it to your clipboard.

Store this token in a secure location. The token is not displayed again after you leave this page. Generate a new one if needed.

Old method (for older server versions)

If you are using an older server version that does not support the direct account settings method, you can generate a PAT via the Swagger UI.

Obtain a temporary bearer token:
First, get a valid bearer token. Follow the steps in the Temporary token section to retrieve it using your browser’s developer tools.

Access Swagger UI:
Navigate to the Swagger UI page: https://<SERVER-URL>/api/swagger-ui/index.html

Authorize with bearer token:
Click the Authorize button and paste your bearer token into the authentication field.

Select personal access token API:
Open the Select a definition dropdown in the top right of the page. Scroll down and select Personal Access Token API.

Generate PAT via POST request:
Use the POST request to generate a new PAT. Optionally, define the number of days the token remains valid. The default is 30 days.

After submitting the request, the PAT appears in the response body:

Save your PAT in a secure location because it is not displayed again after you leave the page. Generate a new one if needed.

Download the model: Bike Model.

Open the SAM editor in your browser and select the desired organization (for example, MyOrga).

Select New Project > SysML V2 > Import File.

Select Choose File for the File to import option.

Select the bike.xmi file that you just downloaded. The project name is automatically set to
bike.

Click Import and wait for the project to load.

You can now work on this bike model.

title: Calculate bike weight > Calculate weight
text:
Calculate weight

The bike weight is the sum of the frame weight and the weight of all wheel component elements.
Thus, you want to calculate the sum of all blue elements in the model:

title: Calculate bike weight > Step 1: Load the project
text:
Step 1: Load the project

Ensure that you have installed PySAM SysML2. If not, see
Installation.

Before loading the project, create a connector and a project manager.

To obtain the required data, see Find organization ID and
Find bearer token.

Import libraries and create a connection to the SysML2 API server

After logging in, load the bike project.

To load a project, you need its ID. Find it in the editor’s URL.

Load the bike project

For more information about the project object, see
Project in the API reference documentation.

text:

title: Write in your model
text:
Write in your model

This is a beta feature and may have some issues.

title: Write in your model > Update a feature value
text:
Update a feature value

You have two functions for updating the value of a feature:

set_value()

parse_and_set_value()

title: Write in your model > Function set_value()
text:
Function set_value()

The set_value() function supports all primitive types:

The model updates after you set all values to ensure accuracy.

title: Write in your model > Function parse_and_set_value()
text:
Function parse_and_set_value()

The parse_and_set_value() function handles more complex
expressions:

title: Write in your model > Create new elements
text:
Create new elements

Use the Factory class to create new elements in your model.

For a comprehensive example, see Create a new element.

Create a Factory instance

Use the create_element() method to create a new model element.
Provide the element type and any number of keyword arguments representing its attributes:

This creates a new AttributeUsage element at the root of your project. The
create_element() method returns the newly created element.

Create a new AttributeUsage element with owner

This creates a new AttributeUsage element with the specified attributes inside the Bike
frame.

The list of accepted attributes depends on the element type you are creating. For example,
name, owner, shortName, and others are defined by the metamodel.

You can also assign a value directly when creating the element. There are two ways:

value=... for simple values (such as numbers).

expression="..." for values with units or expressions.

This lets you set values directly at creation time, depending on your data format.

title: Write in your model > Update attributes directly
text:
Update attributes directly

Update element properties directly using simple assignment. This is useful for quickly changing
properties like names.

title: Load a model
text:
Load a model

To load a model, you need an instance of a SysML Connector. For more information, (see Use a connector).

You can then create an instance of the SysML2ProjectManager class.
This class helps you load a model in Python, using the SysML V2 standard API.

Create a SysML2 project manager instance

For more information, see the SysML2ProjectManager class.

With the project manager, you can load your project like this:

Get the project using the project ID

Once you provide the project ID, you get a Python version of your model.

title: Read your model
text:
Read your model

PySAM SysML2 lets you read and parse the model through a Python script.

The loaded model is stored in a Project object.

title: Read your model > Access methods
text:
Access methods

To parse the project, you can use dot access or underscore access.

title: Read your model > Dot access
text:
Dot access

With dot access, you can access all direct named elements of your SysML element. Also, you can
access some useful top-level functions, such as get_value().

title: Read your model > Sub-elements
text:
Sub-elements

The following code attaches all elements contained in ownedElement and _inheritedFeature,
which have names, to the container element.

Limitations of dot access for sub-elements:

Duplicate names: If multiple elements have the same name, only one is accessible through dot
access. You cannot differentiate between elements with identical names using this method.

Names with spaces: You cannot access elements with spaces in their names (for example, “bike
frame”) using dot notation. Python identifiers cannot contain spaces.

title: Read your model > Function get_value()
text:
Function get_value()

The get_value() function works only for the SysML Feature
element. Use this top-level function to get the value of the feature without reading the internal
structure:

The get_value() function supports:

All primitive types, such as LiteralInteger and LiteralString - Returns the value directly.

Simple expressions, such as <value> [<unit>] - Returns a tuple:
(<value>,<unit short name>).

title: Read your model > Underscore access
text:
Underscore access

With underscore (_) access, you can find all SysML methods:

In this first PySAM SysML2 version, only existing fields (with data) are linked, which means
that you might not find a function that exists in SysML V2.

title: Create a new element
text:
Create a new element

Make sure that you have access to a valid server and a project containing the Bike structure.
If you do not have a project containing the Bike structure, perform the following steps:

Download the model: Bike Model.

Open the SAM editor in your browser and select the desired organization (for example, MyOrga).

Select New Project > SysML V2 > Import File.

Select Choose File for the File to import option.

Select the bike.xmi file that you just downloaded. The project name is automatically set to
bike.

Click Import and wait for the project to load.

title: Create a new element > Create an attribute usage element for the bicycle frame length
text:
Create an attribute usage element for the bicycle frame length

The following code shows how to create a new AttributeUsage element inside the Bike
structure and assign it a value.

You need to replace the organization ID, server URL, and token with your own data. For more
information, see Find information.

Create a new AttributeUsage element using the Factory class

You just created a new element and assigned a parsed value to it.

You can also assign a value directly when creating the element, without using the
set_value() or
parse_and_set_value()
method. There are two ways:

value=... for simple values (such as numbers).

expression="..." for values with units or expressions.

This lets you set values directly at creation time, depending on your data format.
